chunks.text: Michael Ball | EECS at UC Berkeley Michael Ball: Faculty Home Page Larger Photo Michael Ball Continuing Lecturer Michael Ball is a Continuing Lecturer in EECS researching computing pedagogy, educational programming languages, accessibility, and educational software. Contact Information 784 Soda Hall ball@berkeley.edu Office Hours By appointment, please email me., 784 Soda Links Personal Homepage Publications Biography Michael Ball is a Continuing Lecturer in the Department of Electrical Engineering and Computer Sciences at the University of California, Berkeley. He holds a Master of Science in Computer Science from UC Berkeley (2016) and teaches courses in software engineering, introductory programming (CS1), and computing pedagogy and ethics. His research focuses on computing pedagogy, educational programming languages, and the design of accessible educational software. He is a contributor to the Snap! visual programming environment and the Beauty and Joy of Computing (BJC) curriculum, which brings rigorous and engaging CS education to students at the introductory level. His work in educational technology emphasizes broadening participation in computing and improving the usability and accessibility of tools used in learning environments. A frequent contributor to SIGCSE Technical Symposium (the ACM Special Interest Group on Computer Science Education) , Michael regularly presents research on CS pedagogy, curriculum design, and educational software accessibility. His work on web accessibility intersects with both his teaching and research, exploring how tools and platforms â including those used in CS coursework â can meet modern accessibility standards and serve all learners equitably. His work on web accessibility intersects with both his teaching and research, exploring how tools and platforms â including those used in CS coursework â can meet modern accessibility standards and serve all learners equitably. Prior to returning to academia, Michael was an early engineer at Gradescope , an online grading platform that was created by EECS TAs and later acquired by Turnitin, where he contributed to building infrastructure that now supports grading and feedback at institutions worldwide. In his free time, he is a part-time photographer , travel agent, and coffee ethusiast.
